description_long: This object is a vintage biplane with two sets of straight, parallel wings stacked one above the other, connected by struts and wires. The fuselage is relatively short and cylindrical, tapering towards the tail, and features an open cockpit positioned just behind the upper wing. The nose houses a prominent propeller, and the landing gear consists of two main wheels and a small tail skid, typical of early aircraft. The tail section includes a single vertical stabilizer and horizontal stabilizers, forming a conventional tail design. The surface appears to be a mix of dark and metallic tones, suggesting a combination of painted metal and fabric covering. The overall design is reminiscent of early 20th-century aircraft, often seen in historical or display settings.
description: A vintage biplane with stacked wings, open cockpit, front propeller, and classic taildragger landing gear. The surface is dark and metallic, evoking early aviation designs.
description_view_0: Front view showing the dual stacked wings, central propeller, and open cockpit behind the upper wing.
description_view_1: Side view highlighting the short fuselage, open cockpit, and taildragger landing gear with a small tail skid.
description_view_2: Rear view displaying the tail section with a single vertical stabilizer and horizontal stabilizers.
description_view_3: Opposite side view showing the propeller, cockpit placement, and the classic biplane wing structure.
attribute_wingspan_configuration: Biplane configuration with two sets of straight, parallel wings stacked vertically; no winglets or pronounced dihedral/anhedral angles.
attribute_engine_type: Single front-mounted piston engine with a two-blade propeller.
attribute_tail_design: Conventional tail with a single vertical stabilizer and horizontal stabilizers.
attribute_landing_gear_type: Fixed taildragger landing gear with two main wheels and a tail skid.
attribute_cockpit_placement: Open, single-seat cockpit located just behind the upper wing, forward-mounted on the fuselage.
